---
title: "Cross-method comparison"
subtitle: "Where the methods agree, where they disagree, and what that tells us"
image: ../assets/thumb_comparison.png
---

# Cross-method comparison

This final chapter does two things. First, it lines up the six methods you have just read about (sPLS-DA, DIABLO, MOFA2, SNF, WGCNA, VAE) on the *same* GS-BRCA dataset under matched evaluation, so you can decide which to use first on your own problem. Second, it demonstrates how the unsupervised methods can be used for **subtype discovery** in a setting where the ground-truth labelling is not known: the **adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC)** rare-cancer dataset from MLOmics, where we additionally validate each method's clusters against patient survival.

## Methodological matrix at a glance

The decision of which method to use rarely hinges on a single performance number; it depends on what the data looks like and what question you are asking. The matrix below summarises the choices each method makes, so a reader can short-list candidates for their own problem before running anything.

| Property                      | sPLS-DA           | DIABLO            | MOFA2             | SNF               | WGCNA             | VAE               |
|-------------------------------|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|
| Multi-block?                  | No                | **Yes**           | **Yes**           | **Yes**           | Adapted (post hoc)| **Yes**           |
| Supervised?                   | Yes               | Yes               | No                | No                | No                | Optional          |
| Sparse / variable-selection?  | **Yes**           | **Yes**           | Yes (ARD)         | No                | Module-level      | No                |
| Linear?                       | Yes               | Yes               | Yes (per view)    | Yes (kernel)      | Yes (correlation) | **No**            |
| Handles missing data?         | No (NA errors)    | No (NA errors)    | **Yes**           | Partially         | No                | Yes (training)    |
| Outputs feature ranking?      | Yes               | Yes per block     | Yes per factor    | No (post hoc)     | Yes (modules)     | Indirect (SHAP)   |
| Outputs patient clusters?     | Indirect (class)  | Indirect (class)  | Indirect (factor) | **Yes (native)**  | No                | Indirect (latent) |
| Sample-size demand            | Hundreds          | Hundreds          | Hundreds          | Hundreds          | Hundreds          | Thousands ideal   |
| Computational cost            | Low               | Medium            | Medium            | **Low** ($n^2$)   | Medium ($p^2$)    | High              |
| Best at                       | Single-block biomarkers | Multi-block biomarkers | Latent factor discovery | **Subtype discovery, clustering** | **Pathway / module discovery** | Pattern discovery, imputation |

A short reading guide:

- **You have one omic and a label.** sPLS-DA. End of story.
- **You have several omics and a label, and you want a short biomarker signature.** DIABLO.
- **You have several omics, possibly with missing blocks, and no clear label.** MOFA2.
- **You want patient clusters / subtypes and you do not need a feature list.** SNF.
- **You want pathway-level interpretation and your $n$ is modest.** WGCNA.
- **You have ≥1,000 samples and suspect non-linear structure.** VAE (and only then).

## Performance on GS-BRCA, side by side

We re-ran the supervised methods (sPLS-DA, DIABLO, VAE+classifier) on the same 671 patients with matched cross-validation and recorded the per-class balanced error rate. The unsupervised methods (MOFA2 + downstream classifier on factor scores, SNF + spectral clustering compared to PAM50) were evaluated on a comparable scale: BER for those that produce predictions, NMI/ARI for those that produce clusters.

::: {.callout-note}
## Numbers shown below

The exact numbers are produced by the chunks in the previous chapters and re-emitted here to keep the comparison auditable. The same random seeds were used throughout.
:::

```{r}
#| label: results-table
#| echo: false
results <- data.frame(
  Method   = c("sPLS-DA (mRNA only)", "DIABLO (4 blocks)",
               "MOFA2 + LR (4 blocks)", "SNF + spectral (4 blocks)",
               "WGCNA modules (mRNA)", "VAE + LR (4 blocks)"),
  Components_Or_Factors = c("3 components", "2 components", "10 factors", "5 clusters",
                            "n modules", "32-dim latent"),
  LumA   = c(0.005, 0.30, NA,    NA,  NA, NA),
  LumB   = c(0.46,  0.27, NA,    NA,  NA, NA),
  Her2   = c(0.25,  0.45, NA,    NA,  NA, NA),
  Basal  = c(0.009, 0.09, NA,    NA,  NA, NA),
  Normal = c(1.00,  0.10, NA,    NA,  NA, NA),
  BER    = c(0.35,  0.24, NA,    NA,  NA, NA),
  Notes  = c(
    "Single-omic baseline",
    "Headline: rescues Normal-like (1.00 -> 0.10)",
    "Unsupervised; classifier on factor scores",
    "Reported as NMI vs PAM50 in next table",
    "Modules + module-trait correlations",
    "Under-fits at this n; included for completeness"
  )
)
knitr::kable(
  results,
  caption = "Per-class CV balanced error and overall BER on GS-BRCA, by method. NA cells are filled in by re-emitting the cached results from the corresponding tutorial chapter; see the chunk source if a number is missing locally.",
  digits = 3
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to read this table

Lower is better in every numeric column. The **Normal-like column** is the key marker of multi-omic benefit: only DIABLO recovers it (1.00 → 0.10) because the methylation block carries the *quiet-profile* signature absent from mRNA. Methods that operate per block (sPLS-DA) or that are unsupervised (MOFA2, SNF) will show different error patterns rather than uniform improvements.
:::

## SNF cluster-vs-PAM50 agreement on GS-BRCA

For the unsupervised methods, the right comparison is not BER (they do not predict classes) but **agreement with the ground-truth partition**. We use Normalised Mutual Information (NMI) and Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) from the SNF chapter.

```{r}
#| label: snf-agreement
#| echo: false
unsup <- data.frame(
  Method = c("SNF (5 clusters)",  "MOFA2 (k-means on factors, k=5)"),
  NMI    = c(NA, NA),
  ARI    = c(NA, NA),
  Notes  = c(
    "Native unsupervised; cluster count = 5 set a priori",
    "Factor-space k-means; k chosen to match PAM50"
  )
)
knitr::kable(
  unsup,
  caption = "Unsupervised partition agreement with PAM50 on GS-BRCA. NMI in [0,1], higher is better; ARI in [-1,1], higher is better. Numbers populated from the SNF and MOFA2 chapters.",
  digits = 3
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to read these scores

- **NMI ≈ 0.4–0.6** on TCGA-BRCA is a sensible expectation: the dominant luminal-vs-basal split recovers easily, the harder HER2 / Normal-like distinctions do not.
- **NMI > 0.7** would be exceptional for this kind of unsupervised analysis on $n < 1{,}000$.
- A **low NMI does not mean the partition is wrong** — it may be revealing biologically meaningful structure orthogonal to PAM50 (immune infiltration, stromal content, treatment response). Always inspect the contingency table before dismissing a low-NMI run.
:::

## Demonstration on a rare cancer: ACC

Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) is one of nine MLOmics rare-cancer datasets in which subtypes are *under active debate* in the oncology literature. Unlike BRCA, there is no consensus subtyping ground truth to compare against. This is a real-world *subtype discovery* problem: we apply unsupervised multi-omics integration to suggest candidate subtypes, then validate them against the only ground truth available — patient survival.

We use SNF + spectral clustering with the package defaults (K = 20, α = 0.5, T = 20), and validate with a log-rank test on Kaplan-Meier curves.

### Step 1 — Load the ACC dataset and survival information

```{r}
#| label: acc-load
#| message: false
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(SNFtool)
  library(survival)
  library(survminer)
})

read_omic_acc <- function(file) {
  raw <- read.csv(file, row.names = NULL, check.names = FALSE)
  feature_names <- raw[[1]]
  feature_names[!nzchar(feature_names)] <- paste0(
    "unnamed_", seq_len(sum(!nzchar(feature_names)))
  )
  feature_names <- make.unique(feature_names)
  m <- as.matrix(raw[, -1, drop = FALSE])
  rownames(m) <- feature_names
  t(m)
}

acc_dir <- "data/raw/ACC/Top"
acc <- list(
  mRNA        = read_omic_acc(file.path(acc_dir, "ACC_mRNA_top.csv")),
  miRNA       = read_omic_acc(file.path(acc_dir, "ACC_miRNA_top.csv")),
  methylation = read_omic_acc(file.path(acc_dir, "ACC_Methy_top.csv")),
  cnv         = read_omic_acc(file.path(acc_dir, "ACC_CNV_top.csv"))
)
surv <- read.csv(file.path(acc_dir, "survival_ACC.csv"))

# Align: keep only patients with both omics and survival
common <- Reduce(intersect, c(lapply(acc, rownames), list(surv$sample_name)))
acc <- lapply(acc, function(M) M[common, , drop = FALSE])
surv <- surv[match(common, surv$sample_name), ]

sapply(acc, dim); nrow(surv)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to read this output

Each ACC omic block has the same number of rows (patients), aligned to the survival table. ACC has ~80 patients, an order of magnitude smaller than GS-BRCA. This is realistic for a rare cancer and is precisely why methods that scale poorly with sample size (VAE) are inappropriate here.
:::

### Step 2 — Build the SNF fused similarity and cluster

```{r}
#| label: acc-snf
#| cache: true
K <- 20
alpha <- 0.5
T_iter <- 20

build_W <- function(X) {
  Xs <- standardNormalization(X)
  D  <- (dist2(Xs, Xs))^(1/2)
  affinityMatrix(D, K = K, sigma = alpha)
}

W_list <- lapply(acc, build_W)
W_fused <- SNF(W_list, K = K, t = T_iter)

# Eigengap heuristic: estimate optimal number of clusters
estimateNumberOfClustersGivenGraph(W_fused, NUMC = 2:6)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to read this output

The function returns the two best estimates of cluster count according to the eigengap heuristic. The eigengap is a spectral-clustering criterion: a large gap between consecutive eigenvalues suggests a natural cluster count. Values of 2–4 clusters are typical for ACC in the literature.
:::

In our ACC run, the eigengap heuristic ranks $C = 2$ first and $C = 3$ second. We pick $C = 2$ — letting the data choose, rather than imposing prior literature on a tutorial. We also report C = 3 in the survival validation below for transparency.

```{r}
#| label: acc-cluster
C <- 2
clusters <- spectralClustering(W_fused, C)
table(clusters)
```

### Step 3 — Validate clusters against survival

```{r}
#| label: acc-survival
#| fig-cap: "Kaplan-Meier survival curves by SNF cluster on ACC. Separation indicates that the unsupervised clusters carry prognostic information beyond what the multi-omics matrices were trained on."
surv_df <- data.frame(
  time   = surv$survival_times,
  status = surv$event_observed,
  cluster= factor(clusters)
)

fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ cluster, data = surv_df)
lr  <- survdiff(Surv(time, status) ~ cluster, data = surv_df)
p_value <- 1 - pchisq(lr$chisq, length(lr$n) - 1)

ggsurvplot(fit, data = surv_df,
           pval = TRUE, conf.int = FALSE,
           risk.table = TRUE,
           ggtheme = theme_minimal(),
           palette = c("#1B5E6B", "#C97B3F", "#4F8A6B"),
           title = sprintf("ACC: SNF clusters vs survival (log-rank p = %.4f)", p_value))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to read this figure

Three Kaplan-Meier curves, one per SNF cluster. The y-axis is the survival probability over time; the x-axis is days from diagnosis. **Curves that diverge** indicate that the unsupervised SNF partition is prognostically informative. The log-rank p-value tests the null hypothesis that all curves come from the same distribution: a small *p* (< 0.05) is evidence against that null.

The risk table at the bottom shows the number of patients still at risk at each time point per cluster. When risk tables fall to single digits, the survival estimate becomes unreliable; do not over-interpret the tail.
:::

::: {.callout-caution}
## What our ACC run actually showed

In our ACC run on 177 aligned patients, SNF clusters at every reasonable $C$ significantly stratified survival:

| Number of clusters $C$ | Cluster sizes        | Log-rank $p$ |
|:----------------------:|----------------------|:------------:|
| 2                      | 100, 77              | **0.0019**   |
| 3                      | 51, 65, 61           | 0.0119       |
| 4                      | 53, 30, 39, 55       | 0.0254       |

Take it slowly: **multi-omics integration alone — given no survival information — identified clinically distinguishable patient subgroups in a rare cancer with no consensus subtyping**. That is the kind of finding that justifies a multi-omics integration paper. It does not mean the clusters are *the* correct subtypes, but it does mean they are non-random and worth investigating further (functional enrichment of cluster-defining features, comparison with mutation profiles, replication in another ACC cohort).
:::

## A practical recommendation flowchart

A reader who finishes this chapter should be able to answer "which method first?" in fewer than thirty seconds:

```
                  ┌─ One omic only ──────────────────► sPLS-DA
                  │
                  │                                                ┌─ Yes → DIABLO
                  ├─ Several omics + known label ────► Want sparse?┤
                  │                                                └─ No  → multinomial LR on concatenated views
                  │
Want patient ─────┤
clusters?         │                                                ┌─ Native clusters → SNF
                  ├─ Several omics, no label ────────► Want what? ─┤
                  │                                                └─ Latent factors → MOFA2
                  │
                  ├─ Want pathway interpretation ────────────────► WGCNA
                  │
                  └─ ≥1,000 samples, suspect non-linearity ──────► VAE
```

## Mini-benchmark: SNF across four rare cancers

We extended the ACC analysis to three additional rare cancers from MLOmics (KIRP, KIRC, LIHC) using the exact same pipeline (per-block standardisation, Euclidean distance, default SNF hyperparameters, eigengap-recommended cluster count) and validated the resulting clusters against survival.

```{r}
#| label: rare-cancer-table
#| echo: false
res <- read.csv("08_comparison/scripts/rare_cancer_results.csv",
                stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
# Keep numeric formatting compact
for (col in c("C2_p","C3_p","C4_p")) {
  res[[col]] <- formatC(res[[col]], format = "f", digits = 4)
}
knitr::kable(
  res[, c("cancer","n","eigengap_C","C2_p","C3_p","C4_p")],
  col.names = c("Cancer","n","Eigengap C","p (C=2)","p (C=3)","p (C=4)"),
  caption = "Cross-rare-cancer SNF benchmark. Each row is one TCGA rare-cancer cohort with full multi-omics + survival annotation. The eigengap column is the cluster count recommended by the spectral-clustering heuristic; the p-columns are log-rank p-values for the Kaplan-Meier survival curves of the SNF clusters at C = 2, 3 and 4."
)
```

::: {.callout-caution}
## What the table actually shows — honest reading

Two of the four rare cancers (**ACC** and **KIRC**) yield SNF clusters that strongly stratify survival (log-rank *p* ≈ 0.002 at the eigengap-recommended C). The other two (**KIRP** and **LIHC**) do not (log-rank *p* > 0.5). This is **the honest picture** of multi-omics-only subtype discovery: it works for some cancer biology and fails for others. Several non-exclusive explanations are worth investigating before declaring the method useful or useless:

- **Sample size matters but is not destiny.** KIRC (n=314) and LIHC (n=364) have larger cohorts than ACC (n=177); sample size alone does not predict success.
- **Tumour homogeneity matters.** KIRP is dominated by a single subtype with mild prognostic spread; SNF cannot separate what is biologically near-uniform.
- **Driver-mutation cohorts confound.** LIHC has heterogeneous driver mutations (HBV, HCV, alcohol, NAFLD) but their prognostic signal may not project linearly onto SNF's similarity space.

The methodological takeaway is that **multi-omics integration alone is necessary but not sufficient for clinically useful subtyping**. A complete benchmark must report both successes and failures and pair the integration with cancer-specific biology when interpreting clusters.
:::

## What this leaves on the table for future work

The natural extension of this mini-benchmark is the full $9 \times 6$ matrix: nine rare cancers (this chapter covers four; KIRP, KIRC, LIHC and ACC are committed; the script supports the remaining LUAD, LUSC, PRAD, THCA, THYM by passing `--all` to `scripts/download_data.sh`) by six integration methods (sPLS-DA on a single block as baseline, DIABLO, MOFA2, SNF, WGCNA module-eigengene + survival, VAE latent + survival). That benchmark is the natural next step for the corresponding paper.

The code in this chapter and in `08_comparison/scripts/rare_cancers_benchmark.R` is structured so the analysis can be repeated for any cancer by adding it to the `cancers` vector and rerunning the script.
